Adventure 1 A Rude Awakening Thump, Thumpity, Thump The sound coming from my new neighbor's garage at 11 o'clock at night was making it harder and harder for me to concentrate on the spreadsheets staring back at me from my glaring laptop screen. Thump, Thumpity, Thump The distraction was just as well. For the spreadsheets, didn't paint a very pretty picture. Done right depressing, actually. One more depressing was the terse text message I'd received earlier from my store's landlord. I looked at my phone and read it again. Still haven't received rent payment. Now several months out. Will you be continuing in this location? Please advise. Most depressing of all, however, was what I had to tell my oldest daughter just a few minutes before. She had just been accepted to one of the top private colleges in the country, the one she had her heart set on attending since junior high. But being a single dad and trying to keep a failing business afloat had put quite a strain on our finances. And I had been forced to tell her that it looked like she would have to attend a less expensive public college. She tried so hard not to let it show. But her disappointment was clear. I felt awful. One thing was for sure. I needed ideas for turning around my business and I needed them fast. Thump, Thumpity, Thump Ideas. Yes, I needed ideas. But where would they come from? I had already tried everything I knew. I needed new ideas, new ways of thinking and of looking at things. I had known that for some time now, yet I kept coming up blank. Think outside the box, I reminded myself. But inside or outside, the cupboard seemed bare. My inability to come up with ideas was making me more and more pessimistic. And my pessimism seemed to be preventing me from coming up with any ideas. It was a vicious circle and it seemed impossible to get out of. Maybe if I just thump, Thumpity Thump, that did it. It was time to introduce myself to my new neighbor and inform him that the rules of good neighborship did not include making a racket at a time when most good people, whose businesses weren't down the tubes, are getting ready for bed. He opened the side door of his garage with an outstretched hand and an apologetic grin that made it difficult for me to keep my anger stoked to full boil. I'm so sorry, he began. Was all that noise keeping you up? I'm Tom. I shook his hand, about to introduce myself, but he didn't give me a moment. I had an idea, you see, and when I get an idea in my head, I just have to put together a rough model to see if it'll work, no matter the time of day. I looked around his garage. Although it could have held two cars, there were none in sight. Instead, all available space was taken up by hodgepodge of tables and shelves, jam-packed with mechanisms of all shapes and sizes. Each one seemed in a different stage of being assembled or taken apart. It was hard to tell which. Well, I lived next door, I announced, and it's impossible for me to- Sorry, Tom interrupted, holding his hand up. I'm going to have to ask you to not use that word in my workshop. What word? I asked, wondering what kind of a crank I had for a neighbor. The I word. That means something isn't possible. I learned a long time ago that using words like that and talking about what can't be done only eliminates possibilities that might hold valuable solutions. In this room, there is only what is possible. I see, I said, although I didn't really. Then, changing the subject, I added, I heard you're an inventor, are you inventing something? Tom laughed good-naturedly. Well, I suppose you could call me an inventor. But unfortunately, most people think of an inventor as an inveterate tinkerer who's a few wire short of a complete circuit, if you know what I mean. A mad scientist fixated on some idea he thinks will change the world, but that will more than likely go up in a puff of smoke. Both literally, as well as figuratively. So instead, I prefer to think of myself as, and here he seemed to pause somewhat dramatically, a problem solver. I see, and what types of problems do you solve? Well, take this one here for example. He said, picking up a metal tank the size of a large suitcase. It had a large hole cut out of the top, a drain plug at the base, and what seemed to be a small microphone with a wire coming out of it attached to the bottom. A company I work with has a factory that uses an extremely corrosive acid in their manufacturing process. They need to know when the tank that supplies this acid is empty. They've tried fitting the tank with every kind of measuring device known to man, but the acid, and its fumes, make all of them inoperable in a short time. So, they brought the problem to me. What did you do? Well, the first thing I did was define the problem. Define the problem? Isn't that obvious? No, it's not obvious at all. The problem with the way most people go about solving problems is that they aren't clear what the problem really is that they're trying to solve. For example, the people at the factory thought the problem was designing a device that could measure the amount of acid left in the tank and withstand the highly corrosive environment. So, they kept failing. But in talking to them, I was able to define the problem slightly different. I asked, how can the tank tell us when it's empty? And that's a much easier problem to solve. Sure, I said tongue in cheek. All you need to do is design a talking tank. Yes, exactly, he continued, unfazed by my sarcasm. In which case, we don't need measuring devices that are vulnerable to the highly acidic environment. All we need are some hollow acid-resistant steel balls with a lower specific gravity than that of the acid. So, they would float? I ventured. Yes, exactly. So, they would float until the tank runs out of acid. Then, they're designed to roll around the bottom of the tank and make what would technically be called a racket. And that is how the tank tells us that it's empty. He then pulled out the stopper on his model tank. The water quickly drained out into a pan and the little balls began rolling around on the bottom. The sound was quickly picked up by the microphone and amplified over a nearby speaker, making the thumpity thump thump sound that had gotten my attention earlier. As you can see, I mean, as you can hear, I've also used a mic and a speaker to capture, amplify and convey the sound. This way, you don't need someone sitting by the tank all day listening for the balls to begin rattling around. The sound could be conveyed to any office or to the factory's control center. If you wanted to get a little more sophisticated, it would be simple enough to rig the sound to trigger another device, such as an alert on the form and cell. Wow, I said, rocking on my feet. How do you come up with ideas like that? It's simple, he replied. You just define the problem correctly. Often that gives you the answer right there. If not, I very systematically go about finding the solution. A system, huh? I always thought problem solving was unsystematic. Freeform, outside the box. Brainstormy or whatever. Well, I think that's also part of the problem with the way most people go about solving problems. It's very hit or miss. And by trying to think outside the box, all you do is give yourself such a vast area to look in that you're overwhelmed right from the get-go. Instead, I prefer to think inside the box. 15 specific predefined boxes I've come up with to be exact. Thought starters, you might call them. That get you looking for solutions in ways and in places you might not otherwise think to look. To solve this particular problem, he said, holding up the tank, I looked in the first box, the one I call add a new dimension. And in engineering, one of the ways you can add a new dimension is by adding something you can see, hear, feel, or smell. I could have used an optical system. For example, I might have tried to dye the acid and make a side of the tank transparent to see when the acid had run out. However, I wasn't sure acid would hold coloring. And I wasn't sure the acid wouldn't dissolve any transparent tints I could create. That's why I went with something you could hear. The hollow steel balls that make a rattling sound when the acid runs out. That's pretty neat, I replied. I could see where that kind of inside the box thinking would work for mechanical stuff, but can it also be used for non-mechanical problems? Absolutely, Tom replied. When I first learned about this system, I thought of it only in terms of engineering type problems. But the more I worked with it, the more I saw that it could be used to solve other types of problems as well. Everyday problems, personal problems, business problems, you name it. Fascinating. You know, I've got a business problem or two, I said, vastly understating the situation. Would you be open to showing me how I might use your process on my business? Sure, said Tom. You own a bookstore, right? As of right now I do, I answered. Not sure for how much longer though. I took my phone from my pocket and showed him the message from my landlord. Tom read it, nodding. Well then, it looks like we've got no time to waste to get you thinking in some new ways. Okay, let's start from the top. How do you think you could apply the box of add a new dimension to your problem? I could feel my shoulders tensing up as they always did when under pressure to come up with an answer quickly or be creative. Relax, said Tom, picking up on my anxiety. The stress of coming up with new ideas comes from having a big, vast area you have to look through. We're just going to look in one tiny little box and we'll take it one step at a time. As I already told you, the first part of this box is adding the dimension of either sound, feeling, sight, or smell. Let's take them one at a time, applying them to a specific problem that you have. What should we try it on first? Well, I said choosing the first and most obvious problem that came to mind. I'd like to get more customers into my store. Great! Now, how might you get more people into your store using visual, auditory, kinesthetic, which is feeling, or olfactory elements? Now this was something I could wrap my mind around. Let me think. Something you can see. Maybe I could get more people to come in if I had a TV in the sitting area, where the customer or their significant other, who's less partial to reading, could watch Netflix, sports, or the news. Something you could hear, I continued. I could get live music. People love live entertainment. It would bring them in as they walk by, and I could advertise it as an event. It would be so easy to get students or others to play. It's good practice, fun, and gives them great publicity. Something you can feel, prompted Tom, nodding along. Let's see, I could light a fire in the fireplace on colder days. Let the kids toast marshmallows. Offer hot cider. Smell, Tom pushed. That's easy. I've noticed at movie theaters that the smell of popcorn really carries. I bet if I popped some popcorn, the aroma would waft out to the street and draw people in. And I could advertise that we provide free popcorn to anyone who enters the store. People love free stuff. And another thing I could do is, okay, okay, Tom interrupted. I think you get the idea. And that's just the first part of this box. You can also explore other dimensions by adding two or replacing things. You can work with the dimension of movement, by making an object or process that moves stationary. Or making one that stationary move. You can make something change over time or stop something from changing. And you can go from random to ordered, or from ordered to random. Stationary to moving, I repeated slowly. That doesn't really apply to book selling. Tom raised his eyebrows, challenging me. Although now that I think about it, books shelved in a bookstore are very stationary. Which can be limiting. Why couldn't I find a way to take books to people at different events? Like a little kiosk selling sports books at sporting events. Business books where business groups and associations have meetings. That could open up whole new markets for us. What was next? Turn something unchanging into something that changes? Tom smiled approvingly. The menu in our cafe hasn't changed since we opened it. Why couldn't there be at least one or two new weekly specials? People might come in just to see what we have. Random to ordered, Tom offered. I thought for a moment. It's random the way people come into the store. And whatever salesperson happens to be available serves them. What if this process were more ordered? Say by assigning a specific salesperson to each of our best customers. That way the customer could call anytime and their personal salesperson or a book concierge could keep them informed of when new books of interest are coming in. Really getting the hang of this said Tom. Now when you look inside this box in the future, don't just limit yourself to the dimensions we've discussed. The senses, movement, change and so on. Feel free to add your own and look at or emphasize a new dimension of any object, part or process. I felt like a long suffering prospector who had finally found an infallible goal detector that could spot gold wherever it was hidden. I don't know when I've been able to come up with so many new ideas. For goodness sake, I exclaimed. What are all the other boxes? Don't worry, said my new friend. It's late and they'll still be here tomorrow. Why don't you stop by in the evening and then we can continue our little discussion. You can count on it. Oh, and by the way, come hungry, he winked.
